risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were randomly assigned to treatment group, stratified by race. Computer randomization occurred after the collection of baseline data." Comment: The study explicitly states that allocation was done by computer randomization, which is a truly random method. Although the paper does not describe the exact algorithm, computer randomization is sufficient here to judge the sequence generation as low risk. Stratification by race does not weaken this judgement because the randomization method itself is stated.

Quote: "Participants were randomly assigned to treatment group, stratified by race. Computer randomization occurred after the collection of baseline data." Comment: The paper reports random assignment and computer randomization, but gives no explicit information about how the allocation sequence was concealed from the person enrolling participants. Because computer-generated randomization describes sequence generation rather than concealment, foreknowledge of the next assignment cannot be ruled out.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A prospective randomized controlled trial with two parallel interventions was employed." ... "A dietitian led all cohorts of the DSME intervention, and the same dietitian and a social worker with extensive training in mindful meditation co-led all cohorts of the MB-EAT intervention." ... "No information regarding mindful eating or meditation was presented." Comment: The report describes two distinct behavioral group interventions with different content and facilitators, and it does not state that participants or personnel were blinded. Because the outcomes included self-reported diet, self-efficacy, anxiety, depression, and mindfulness, knowledge of assignment could plausibly influence both participant behavior and outcome reporting, so performance bias is likely high.

Quote: "The valid 110-item Block 2005 Food Frequency Questionnaire ... was self-administered to assess usual intake" and "Anxiety and depression can be triggers for overeating... The 21-item Beck Anxiety Inventory... The 21-item Beck Depression Inventory–II... The Five-Facet Mindfulness Questionnaire... was administered" Comment: The main outcomes included multiple patient self-report measures (dietary intake, anxiety, depression, self-efficacy, mindfulness), so the patient was effectively the outcome assessor for many endpoints. The paper does not report blinding of participants or outcome assessors, and because these are subjective outcomes that can be influenced by knowledge of allocation, lack of blinding could bias outcome measurement.

Quote: "Thirty-two participants were randomized to MB-EAT-D; 27 received the allocated intervention and completed data collection. Thirty-six were randomized to SC; 25 received the allocated intervention and completed data collection. There was no significant difference in attrition between treatment groups (p > .05)... Participants with at least two observed measures were included in the analyses. There were few missing values (≤9%); the analyses did not impute data except for the TFEQ, where missing values were imputed by the first observed value." Comment: Attrition was modest overall (5/32 and 11/36 randomized did not complete, about 16% and 31% of randomized; among those who received treatment and completed data collection, the comparison groups were still analyzed with low missingness at the outcome level, reported as ≤9%). The paper explicitly states no significant difference in attrition between groups, and missing data were limited with a mixed-effects model and minimal imputation, making bias from incomplete outcome data unlikely.
